blocks:
Ayahs

إِذَا جَآءَ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْفَتْحُ (١)
When Allah’s help comes with victory, (1)
وَرَأَيْتَ ٱلنَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ أَفْوَاجًا (٢)
and you see the people entering Allah’s religion in throngs, (2)
فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ تَوَّابًۢا (٣)
celebrate the praise of your Lord, and plead to Him for forgiveness. Indeed, He is all-clement. (3)

Content and Virtue of Surah An-Nasr

This Surah was revealed in Medina after the Migration (Hijrah).### Content and Virtue of Surah An-Nasr

This Surah was revealed in Medina after the Hijrah. It conveys glad tidings and the promise of a great victory, following which people would enter the religion of Allah in crowds. Therefore, to show gratitude for this great blessing, it invites the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to "glorify" (Tasbih), "praise" (Hamd) Allah, and seek "forgiveness" (Istighfar).

Although many conquests occurred in Islam, no victory possessed the aforementioned characteristics except the "Conquest of Mecca." This is especially true since, according to some narrations, the Arabs believed that if the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) conquered Mecca and gained mastery over it, it would be proof of his truthfulness; for if he were not on the side of truth, Allah would not grant him such permission, just as He did not permit the massive army of "Abrahah." For this reason, after the Conquest of Mecca, the Arab polytheists entered Islam in crowds.

Some have said that this Surah was revealed after the "Treaty of Hudaybiyyah" in the sixth year of the Hijrah, two years before the "Conquest of Mecca."

However, the possibility suggested by some that it was revealed after the Conquest of Mecca in the tenth year of the Hijrah during the Farewell Pilgrimage (Hajjat al-Wada') is very unlikely. The expressions in the Surah are inconsistent with this meaning, as it gives news of a future event, not a past one.

One of the names of this Surah is Surah "Tawdi'" (Tawdi' means "bidding farewell"), because it contains an implicit notification of the passing of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

It is mentioned in a Hadith that when this Surah was revealed and the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) recited it to his companions, they all became happy and cheerful. However, Abbas, the uncle of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), wept upon hearing it. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asked: "O uncle, why do you weep?"

He replied: "I think that the news of your passing has been given in this Surah, O Messenger of Allah!" He (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "The matter is as you say." [^1] There is discussion among commentators as to which part of the Surah implies this, as the literal meaning of the verses contains nothing but glad tidings of conquest and victory. This concept is apparently derived from the fact that this Surah indicates that the mission of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) has reached its conclusion and his religion has been fully established. It is understood that in such a state, the expectation of departing from the transient abode to the eternal world is entirely predictable.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it is mentioned in a Hadith (from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله):

"Whoever recits it, it is as if he was present with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) at the Conquest of Mecca." [^2] In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "Whoever recites Surah 'Idha Ja'a Nasru-llahi wal-Fath' in his recommended or obligatory prayers, Allah will grant him victory over all his enemies, and on the Day of Resurrection, he will enter the gathering with a document that speaks; Allah will have sent it out with him from his grave, and it is a guarantee of safety from the fire of Hell..." ^3

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 554. This theme is mentioned in numerous narrations with various wordings (Al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 532). [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 553.

It goes without saying that all this honor and virtue belong to the one who, by reading this Surah, places himself on the path of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) and acts according to his religion and Sunnah, rather than sufficing with mere lip service.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

إِذا جاءَ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ وَ الْفَتْحُ (1) وَ رَأَيْتَ النَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ أَفْواجاً (2) فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَ اسْتَغْفِرْهُ إِنَّهُ كانَ تَوَّاباً (3)

Commentary

When the Final Victory Arrives!

In the first verse of this Surah, He says: "When the help of Allah and the victory come" (إِذا جاءَ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ وَ الْفَتْحُ).

"And you see the people entering the religion of Allah in crowds" (وَ رَأَيْتَ النَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ، أَفْواجاً).

"Then glorify the praise of your Lord and seek His forgiveness, for He is ever-Acceptor of Repentance" (فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَ اسْتَغْفِرْهُ إِنَّهُ كانَ تَوَّاباً).

In these three short yet profound verses, there are many subtleties that help in understanding the ultimate goal of the Surah:

1- In the first verse, "help" (Nasr) is attributed to "Allah" (Nasru-llah). This attribution is not only found here; it is reflected in many verses of the Quran. For instance, in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 214, we read: "Unquestionably, the help of Allah is near" (أَلا إِنَّ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ).

And in Surah Al-Imran, verse 126, and Al-Anfal, verse 10, it says: "And victory is not but from Allah" (وَ مَا النَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ).

This indicates that, in any case, help and victory are by the Will of the Truth (Allah).

It is true that to overcome the enemy, one must provide forces and prepare strength, but a monotheist (Muwahhid) considers victory to come only from Allah. For this reason, he does not become arrogant at the time of victory; rather, he engages in thanking and praising Allah.

2- In this Surah, mention is first made of divine help, then the opening and victory, and then the influence and expansion of Islam and the entry of people into the religion of Allah in groups. These three are the cause and effect of one another: as long as there is no divine help, there is no opening or victory; and until victory arrives and obstacles are removed from the path, people do not become Muslim in crowds. Naturally, following these three stages—each of which is a great blessing—comes the fourth stage: the stage of gratitude, praise, and glorification of Allah.

On the other hand, divine help and victory are all for the sake of achieving the ultimate goal, which is the entry of people into the religion of Allah and universal guidance.

3- "The Victory" (Al-Fath) is mentioned here in an absolute sense, and with the clues previously mentioned, it undoubtedly refers to the Conquest of Mecca, which had such a widespread impact. Truly, the Conquest of Mecca opened a new chapter in the history of Islam, as the main center of polytheism was dismantled, idols were destroyed, the hope of idol-worshippers turned to despair, and the obstacles in the way of people's faith in Islam were removed.

For this reason, the Conquest of Mecca must be considered the stage of the consolidation of Islam and its establishment in the Arabian Peninsula, and subsequently in the world. Therefore, after the Conquest of Mecca, no further resistance was seen from the polytheists (except in one case which was quickly suppressed), and people from all parts of the peninsula came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to accept Islam.

4- At the end of the third verse, three important commands are given to the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) (and naturally to all believers), which are in fact a thanksgiving for this great victory and an appropriate reaction to this divine help: the commands to "glorify" (Tasbih), "praise" (Hamd), and "seek forgiveness" (Istighfar).

"Tasbih" means considering Allah free from any defect or flaw, "Hamd" is for describing Him with attributes of perfection, and "Istighfar" is in response to the shortcomings and deficiencies of the servants.

This great victory caused polytheistic thoughts to be erased, the perfection and beauty of Allah to become more apparent, and those who had lost their way to return to the Truth.

This massive conquest caused people not to think that Allah leaves His allies alone (purity from this defect) and also to know that Allah is capable of fulfilling His promises (being described with this perfection), and also for the servants to confess their own deficiency before His greatness.

Furthermore, it is possible that at the time of victory, undesirable reactions may arise in a person, such as falling into "arrogance and self-superiority," or seeking "revenge and personal settlement of accounts" against the enemy. These three commands teach him that at the sensitive moment of victory, he should remember the attributes of Allah's Majesty and Beauty, attribute everything to Him, and engage in seeking forgiveness so that both his pride and negligence are vanished, and he remains far from seeking revenge.

5- It is certain that the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), like all prophets, was "infallible" (Ma'sum); so what is the command to seek forgiveness for?

In answer to this question, it must be said that this is a model for the entire Ummah because:

Firstly: Throughout this long struggle which lasted many years (about twenty years) and during which Muslims went through very difficult and painful days, sometimes events became so complicated that souls reached the throats, and in the minds of some, bad thoughts about divine promises arose, just as the Quran says regarding the Battle of the "Trench" (Ahzab): "And the hearts reached the throats, and you were harboring [various] thoughts about Allah" (Al-Ahzab: 10).

Now that victory has arrived, they realize that all those thoughts and anxieties were wrong, and they must engage in "seeking forgiveness."

Secondly: No matter how much a human praises and glorifies Allah, he will still not fulfill the right of gratitude to Him; therefore, at the end of this praise and glorification, one must "turn to the threshold of Allah" for his shortcomings and seek forgiveness.

Thirdly: Usually, after victories, the temptations of Satan begin, and a state of "arrogance" on one hand, and extremism and revenge-seeking on the other, emerge. Here, one must remember Allah and constantly seek forgiveness so that none of these states arise, or if they have, they are removed.

Fourthly: As we said at the beginning of the Surah, the announcement of this victory was almost an announcement of the end of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) mission, the completion of his life, and his hastening to meet the Beloved. This state is appropriate for "Tasbih," "Hamd," and "Istighfar." Therefore, it is mentioned in narrations that after the revelation of this Surah, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) repeated this phrase frequently:

"Glory be to You, O Allah, and with Your praise. O Allah! Forgive me, for You are the ever-Acceptor of Repentance, the Merciful." [^1]

6- The phrase "إِنَّهُ كانَ تَوَّاباً" (for He is ever-Acceptor of Repentance) is an explanation of the reason for the matter of seeking forgiveness, meaning:

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 554.

"Seek forgiveness and repent, for Allah is very accepting of repentance."

Additionally, it perhaps points to the fact that when Allah accepts your repentance, you too should, as much as possible, accept the repentance of the wrongdoers after victory, and as long as no contrary decision or signs of conspiracy appear from them, do not drive them away. Thus, as we shall see, in this very event of the Conquest of Mecca, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) demonstrated the face of Islamic kindness and mercy toward the defeated, spiteful enemies in the most sublime manner.

It was not only the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) who thought of glorification, praise, and seeking forgiveness upon final victory over the enemy; rather, this matter is clearly evident in the history of other prophets as well.

For example, when Prophet Joseph (عليه السلام) sat upon the throne of Egypt's government, and his parents and brothers were reunited with him after a long separation, he said:

"My Lord, You have given me [something] of sovereignty and taught me of the interpretation of dreams. Creator of the heavens and earth, You are my protector in this world and the Hereafter. Cause me to die a Muslim and join me with the righteous" (Yusuf: 101).

And the Prophet of Allah, Solomon (عليه السلام), when he saw the throne of the Queen of Sheba present before him, said: "This is from the favor of my Lord to test me whether I will be grateful or ungrateful" (An-Naml: 40).

Key Point: The Conquest of Mecca, the Greatest Victory of Islam

The Conquest of Mecca, as we mentioned, opened a new chapter in the history of Islam and broke the enemy's resistance after about twenty years. In truth, with the Conquest of Mecca, the carpet of polytheism and idol-worship was rolled up from the Arabian Peninsula, and Islam became ready to leap toward other countries of the world.

The summary of this event was as follows: After the Treaty and Peace of Hudaybiyyah, the polytheists of Mecca engaged in breaking the treaty and ignored that peace agreement, putting pressure on some of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) allies. The allies of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) complained to him, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) decided to help his allies.

On the other hand, all conditions were being prepared to dismantle this center of idol-worship, polytheism, and hypocrisy that had been created in Mecca, and this was a task that had to be done eventually. Therefore, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), by the command of Allah, prepared to move toward Mecca.

The Conquest of Mecca took place in three stages: First, the preparatory stage, which involved providing the necessary forces and power, choosing favorable timing, and gathering sufficient information about the enemy's position and the quantity and quality of their physical and spiritual strength. The second stage was the stage of performing the conquest very skillfully and without casualties. Finally, the final stage was the stage of its consequences and effects.

1- This stage was carried out with the utmost precision and delicacy. Specifically, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) so strictly controlled the road between Mecca and Medina that news of this great preparation did not reach the Meccans at all. Consequently, they made no preparations and were completely taken by surprise. This very factor caused almost no blood to be shed in that holy land during this massive invasion and great victory.

Even a weak-faith Muslim named "Hatib bin Abi Balta'ah," who wrote a letter to the Quraysh and sent it secretly to Mecca with a woman from Ta'if (Mazina) named "Kafud" or "Sarah," was revealed to the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) through miraculous means. Ali (عليه السلام) and some others were quickly dispatched after her, and they found the woman at one of the stations between Mecca and Medina, took the letter from her, and returned her to Medina—the story of which was explained in detail under the first verse of Surah Al-Mumtahanah. [^1] In any case, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) appointed a deputy over Medina and moved toward Mecca on the tenth of Ramadan in the eighth year of the Hijrah, arriving in Mecca after ten days.

On the way, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) saw his uncle Abbas, who was coming from Mecca toward him as a migrant (Muhajir). The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to him: "Send your belongings to Medina and come with us yourself, for you are the last of the migrants."

2- Finally, the Muslims reached the vicinity of Mecca and camped outside the city in the surrounding plains at a place called "Marr al-Zahran," which was only a few kilometers from Mecca. At night, they lit many fires to prepare food (and perhaps to demonstrate their widespread presence). A group of the people of Mecca saw this sight and were struck with wonder.

News of the movement of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the army of Islam was still hidden from the Quraysh. That night, Abu Sufyan, the leader of the Meccans, and some other leaders of polytheism came out of Mecca to track news. At this moment, Abbas, the Prophet's uncle, thought that if the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) entered Mecca by force, no one from the Quraysh would survive. He took permission from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and mounted his beast, saying: "I am going; perhaps I will see someone to tell him to inform the people of Mecca of the situation so they may come and seek amnesty."

Abbas moved and came closer. By chance, at this moment, he heard the voice of "Abu Sufyan" saying to one of his friends named "Budayl": "I have never seen a fire greater than this!" Budayl said: "I think these fires belong to the tribe of Khuza'ah." Abu Sufyan said: "The tribe of Khuza'ah is too lowly to light all these fires!" Here, Abbas called out to Abu Sufyan. Abu Sufyan recognized Abbas and said: "What is the news?"

Abbas replied: "This is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) who has come for you with ten thousand soldiers of Islam!" Abu Sufyan became severely panicked and said: "What do you command me to do?"

Abbas said: "Come with me and seek amnesty from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), for otherwise you will be killed!" Thus, Abbas took Abu Sufyan with him on the Messenger of Allah's (صلى الله عليه وآله) mount and returned quickly to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). As he passed by every group and every fire, they would say: "This is the Prophet's uncle riding his mount; he is not a stranger," until he reached where Umar ibn al-Khattab was. When Umar's eyes fell upon Abu Sufyan, he said: "Thanks be to Allah who has given me power over you (Abu Sufyan) while you have no amnesty!" He immediately went to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and asked for permission to behead Abu Sufyan.

But Abbas arrived and said: "O Messenger of Allah! I have given him protection."

The Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "I also grant him amnesty for now until you bring him to me tomorrow."

The next day, when Abbas brought him to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to him: "Woe to you, O Abu Sufyan! Has the time not come for you to believe in the One God?"

He said: "Yes, may my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah! I bear witness that Allah is One and has no partner. If the idols could have done anything, I would not have fallen into this state!" He (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Has the time not come for you to know that I am the Messenger of Allah?!" He said: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, there is still some doubt in my heart." But eventually, Abu Sufyan and two of his companions became Muslim.

The Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to Abbas: "Take Abu Sufyan to the narrow pass that is the gateway to Mecca so that the divine armies may pass by there and he may see them."

Abbas said: "Abu Sufyan is an ambitious man; grant him some distinction." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Whoever enters the house of Abu Sufyan is safe, and whoever seeks refuge in the Sacred Mosque (Masjid al-Haram) is safe, and whoever stays in his own house and closes the door upon himself is also safe."

In any case, when Abu Sufyan saw this massive army, he became certain that no way for confrontation remained. He turned to Abbas and said: "The kingdom of your nephew has become very great!" Abbas said: "Woe to you, it is not a kingdom; it is Prophethood."

Then Abbas said to him: "Go quickly to the people of Mecca and warn them against confronting the army of Islam!" Abu Sufyan entered the Sacred Mosque and shouted: "O people of Quraysh! Muhammad has come to you with a multitude that you have no power to confront!" Then he added: "Whoever enters my house is safe, whoever goes into the Sacred Mosque is safe, and whoever closes his door upon himself will be safe."

Then he shouted: "O people of Quraysh! Accept Islam so that you may remain safe!" His wife, Hind, grabbed his beard and shouted: "Kill this foolish old man!" Abu Sufyan said: "Let go! By Allah, if you do not accept Islam, you too will be killed. Go inside the house."

Then the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) moved with the ranks of the Islamic army until he reached the point of "Dhi Tuwa," the high point from which the houses of Mecca are visible. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) remembered the day he was forced to leave Mecca secretly, but now he saw himself entering Mecca with such greatness. Therefore, he placed his blessed forehead on the saddle of the camel and performed a prostration of gratitude.

Then the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) descended at "Hajun" (one of the elevated neighborhoods of Mecca where Khadijah's grave is located), performed a bath (Ghusl), and mounted his beast in battle dress and arms. While reciting Surah "Al-Fath," he entered the Sacred Mosque and said "Allahu Akbar." The army of Islam also all said "Allahu Akbar," such that their voices filled all the plains and mountains.

Then he dismounted from his camel and approached the Kaaba to destroy the idols. He overturned the idols one after another, saying: "Truth has come, and falsehood has departed. Indeed, falsehood is [by nature] ever bound to depart."

Several large idols were installed atop the Kaaba where the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) hand could not reach. He commanded the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), to place his feet on his blessed shoulders and climb up to throw the idols to the ground and break them. Ali (عليه السلام) obeyed this command.

Then he took the key to the Kaaba, opened the door, and erased the pictures of the prophets that were drawn on the inner walls of the Kaaba.

3- After this brilliant and swift victory, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) held the ring of the Kaaba's door and turned to the people of Mecca who were gathered there and said:

"What do you say? And what do you think?! What 'command should I give' regarding you?"

They said: "We expect nothing but good and kindness from you. You are our noble brother and the son of our noble brother! And today you have come to power; forgive us." Tears welled up in the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) eyes, and the sound of the people of Mecca weeping also rose.

The Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "I say regarding you the same as my brother Joseph said: 'No blame will there be upon you today. Allah will forgive you; and He is the most merciful of the merciful.'" [^1] And thus he pardoned them all and said: "You are all free; go wherever you wish."

In addition, the Prophet of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) had commanded his soldiers not to disturb anyone and that absolutely no blood should be shed. According to one narration, he had only excepted six people who were very foul-mouthed and dangerous individuals.

Even when he heard that "Sa'd bin Ubadah," the standard-bearer of the army, had raised the slogan of revenge, saying: "Today is the day of slaughter (Al-Yawm yawm al-malhamah)!", the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to Ali (عليه السلام): "Hasten, take the flag from him and be the standard-bearer yourself, and shout: 'Today is the day of mercy (Al-Yawm yawm al-marhamah)'!" Thus, Mecca was conquered without bloodshed, and the attraction of this Islamic pardon and mercy—which they never expected—so affected their hearts that people came in crowds and became Muslim. The news of this great victory echoed throughout all the islands of Arabia, and the fame of Islam spread everywhere. The position of Islam and the Muslims was consolidated in every respect. [^1] In some histories, it is mentioned that when the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) stood by the door of the Kaaba, he said:

"There is no god but Allah alone, alone. He fulfilled His promise, helped His servant, and He alone defeated the confederates. Know that every wealth, every distinction, or every blood claim is under these two feet of mine!..."

(Meaning: Do not speak anymore of the blood that was shed in the time of ignorance, or the wealth that was looted. All the distinctions of the era of ignorance have also been invalidated, and thus all the files of the past were closed).

This was a very important and extraordinary plan which, combined with the general amnesty decree, severed the people of Hijaz from their dark and eventful past and gave them a new life under the light of Islam, which was completely free from the conflicts and controversies related to the past.

And this extraordinary act helped the progress of Islam and is a model for our today and tomorrow.

O Allah! You are capable of returning that ancient greatness to the Muslims once again in the light of following the Sunnah of that Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

O Lord! Place us among the true companions of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله).

O God! Grant us the success to spread the government of Islamic justice in the world such that the people of the world accept it from their hearts and souls in crowds.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah An-Nasr.

[^1]: Surah Yusuf, verse 92. [^1]: Summarized from Kamil ibn Athir, Vol. 2, and Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the verses discussed, and other books.
